JB here, the wolf is in the house for your daily motivation slash skill training. Let me ask you a question. Do you know about following up? Are you proficient at that? Do you really know what to do when you can't close someone on that first call or third call, whatever that, you know, that closing call is? You know in many industries you shouldn't be trying to close on the first or second call. It just might not be rational. There's a longer sale cycle. What I typically see, just so you know, with these longer sale cycles, is there about products that eat integration. You can't just buy the product and like it's you know just work but works. It's got to be fitting in with other systems around a company, other people using it. So it requires other minds to meet. So the sale cycle is a bit longer, right? Now of course with the straight line system we do whatever we can to shorten that cycle and we typically can. But a sale cycle is meant to be what it's meant to be. You know there's a point of diminishing returns just as well. You shorten it too much, you're gonna get some angry people who will hang up on you or stop taking your calls. So you don't want to pressure them to the point where you get some knocks, you're not losing sales, but you certainly don't want to let things just organically drag out because in the absence of some intervention with the straight line, the sales side could just drag and drag and drag. You know people as I always say they're not waiting on the other end of the phone or at their door for you to knock. You know I'm only get my bag of money, I just had it waiting for you. Here you go. It's not the way things work. People are going about their life, they face challenges, right? And you come into their world and now you're gonna try to sell them something that's gonna make their life better. When I talk about following up, what I mean is that you get to this point in the sale, and this is, it's in the mod file, it's less than 11 to 12 hours. You know when do you end the call and how do you essentially set it up so that you can call back and not piss the person off or should they call you back? Listen, if you're not in certification, shame on you. It's a life changing program. So I'm going to speak to you as if you're on certification because you really should be and if you're not, you're lost, all right? There's a magic moment in every sale where you say to yourself, you know what? I'm at that point where if I push harder, I'm going to alienate the prospect to the point where it becomes destructive to long-term relationship they should buy from me and also just starts to make it more difficult to close them. You're better off to begin a second call. Question, when you have that situation, do you want to set it up so you call the prospect back or they call you back? You understand that? In other words, there's different options, different permutations. You could say, you know what? I want to just want you to think about it. Do your research. I'll say some more information, okay? And I'm around next week Thursday. If I want you to give me a call back, when you have a bit more time, right? And we could discuss things then. Does that sound good? That's one way, right? Or you could say, great, let me send you out the information. You can do your research. Great. Now, I'm actually in the office. I'm free between Tuesday at 2 to 6 and Wednesday in the morning. Which day is better for you? I'll give you a call. Which one do you prefer? Two radically different approaches, right? One, you're putting them in the driver's seat. One, you're in the driver's seat. Why would you ever do the first one? Where you put them in the driver's seat? Obviously, your amount of people, your speech was going to radically drop, right? So why would you do that? There's a reason. You do that when you find out that after I've tracked all my follow-up calls, after 1,000 conversations, I've closed one or four. Okay? That is not something I might be kidding you. You know, in the stock market, if they wanted to call back, if you couldn't get them on that first call, you know what percentage you'd buy from you? Zero. They never bought on the second call. And there are many industries that it's the exact opposite. Second, third call, they just buy right there. So how do you know what to do? Well, you got to test this. If you're in a situation where you're essentially doing callbacks and no one's buying from you, shift it and only speak to those that call you back. Now you don't even really just a buyer.
